abstract: Traditional transition-based dependency parsers rely heavily on manual feature engineering, typically resulting in millions of sparse indicator features. These systems suffer from high computational overhead during feature extraction and poor generalization to unseen data. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural network-based classifier designed for a greedy, transition-based dependency parser. Our approach represents words, part-of-speech tags, and dependency labels as low-dimensional dense vectors. By utilizing a compact set of these dense features and a novel cube activation function to capture high-order interactions, our model achieves significant improvements in both accuracy and efficiency. Experimental results on English and Chinese datasets demonstrate that our parser increases unlabeled and labeled attachment scores by approximately 2% over traditional baselines. Notably, the system is capable of parsing more than 1,000 sentences per second while maintaining an unlabeled attachment score of 92.2% on the English Penn Treebank, providing a powerful balance of speed and precision.
